title: Data centers' energy demand threatens Trump's "Made in America" plan
gist: AI data center demand in the 13-state PJM Interconnection grid has driven capacity prices up more than 11x since 2024, squeezing Rust Belt steelmakers and brick factories with sharply higher power bills, undercutting Trump's 'Made in America' manufacturing goal even as clean-energy cancellations worsen the supply crunch.

insights.claim: Factory electricity bills in PJM territory are rising faster than for other business or residential customers, with Ohio's 141-year-old Belden Brick Company seeing monthly bills soar from $1,600 to $12,000 due to higher capacity charges.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Illustrates how data-center-driven capacity charges directly compress industrial margins, a cost input to watch for US manufacturers.
insights.quote: whose electricity bills have soared from $1,600 to $12,000 per month due to a higher monthly capacity charge
insights.locator: Factory electricity bills are generally rising

insights.claim: Ohio steelmaker Metallus said its electricity costs jumped 70% since 2024, adding an extra $15 million in annual energy costs, while the Steel Manufacturers Association warned US steel firms in PJM face tens of millions in higher power costs yearly.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Quantifies a direct hit to steel-industry profitability, relevant to US steel equity margins and pricing.
insights.quote: described its electricity costs as having jumped by 70 percent since 2024, leading the company to pay an extra $15 million in energy costs annually

insights.claim: Electricity accounts for 20 to 40 percent of total steel production costs, and each electric arc furnace draws 40-200 MW with the US steel industry pulling up to 11 GW at peak, making steelmakers acutely exposed to power-price spikes.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: High electricity intensity makes steel a leveraged bet on grid power prices; rising capacity costs flow straight to the P&L.
insights.quote: Electricity accounts for 20 to 40 percent of the total production costs of making steel.

insights.claim: Some manufacturers are raising customer prices to offset power bills or considering relocation, and steel executives warn production outages become more likely if local grids are overwhelmed.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Second-order effects: inflation pass-through, capacity flight, and supply disruption risk in energy-intensive manufacturing.
insights.quote: production outages could become more likely if local power grids are overwhelmed by demand

insights.claim: PJM capacity prices rose from $28.92 per megawatt-day in 2024 to $329.17 per megawatt-day in 2026, an increase of more than 11x driven by AI data center demand.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A more than tenfold capacity-price jump is a structural cost signal for anyone drawing power in PJM's 13-state footprint.
insights.quote: from $28.92 per megawatt-day in 2024 to $329.17 per megawatt-day in 2026
insights.locator: The higher electricity costs for manufacturers coincide

insights.claim: PJM forecasts electricity demand will exceed available supply by 6.6 gigawatts starting in 2027, equivalent to more than six nuclear power plants.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A structural supply deficit points to sustained high power prices and reliability risk in the region through the AI buildout.
insights.quote: electricity demand in its territory will surpass available supply by 6.6 gigawatts starting in 2027

insights.claim: Data center construction consumes an estimated 1 million tons of US steel per year, so steelmakers simultaneously benefit from data-center demand while being harmed by the resulting power-cost inflation.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: The AI buildout is both a demand tailwind and a cost headwind for steel, complicating the net investment thesis.
insights.quote: US steelmakers have benefited from data center construction's requirements for an estimated 1 million tons of steel per year.

insights.claim: The US saw 266 gigawatts of power projects cancelled in 2025, equal to 25% of current US generation capacity and more than all of Texas's generation, with clean-energy projects accounting for 93% of the cancellations.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Massive cancelled capacity worsens the supply deficit precisely as demand surges, reinforcing a bullish setup for power prices.
insights.quote: the cancellation of power projects totaling 266 gigawatts of generation capacity—equivalent to 25 percent of America's current electricity generation capacity

insights.claim: The author argues the Trump administration's efforts to stop wind and solar projects have worsened the supply shortfall, contradicting its stated 'Made in America' manufacturing revival goal.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Frames an explicit policy tension: pro-AI and anti-renewable stances collide with manufacturing competitiveness.
insights.quote: The Trump administration's efforts to stop renewable energy projects involving wind and solar power have also not helped.
insights.locator: But the United States still faces

insights.claim: The White House's Ratepayer Protection Pledge getting Big Tech to fund new power infrastructure lacks any meaningful enforcement mechanism, per the author.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Suggests voluntary cost-shifting to hyperscalers is unlikely to materially relieve ratepayers or manufacturers.
insights.quote: signing a Ratepayer Protection Pledge, which happens to lack any meaningful enforcement mechanism

insights.claim: The US lost 83,000 manufacturing jobs in Trump's first year back in office, undercutting the administration's manufacturing-priority claims even before power-cost pressures.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Baseline weakness plus rising energy costs compounds pressure on US industrial competitiveness.
insights.quote: despite the loss of 83,000 manufacturing jobs in Trump's first year back in office
